Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Figure 1a explicitly labels RPX1 as At5g18110.

Applied_Species.reason: The study organism is Arabidopsis throughout; RPX1 was tested in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The title and text explicitly refer to Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Wild-type control and transformation background are Col-0.

Source_Species.reason: RPX1 coding/genomic sequences were amplified from Col-0 Arabidopsis.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The source species is Arabidopsis thaliana as stated in the paper.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Constructs were made from Col-0 genomic DNA or cDNA.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results sections 3.5 and 3.6 directly link RPX1 inactivation to accumulation of DMNT.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The abstract explicitly calls DMNT the homoterpene (3E)-4,8-dimethyl-1,3,7-nonatriene; terpenoid homoterpene is consistent with the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Results show DMNT treatment suppresses larval growth and damages the peritrophic matrix/midgut, contributing to resistance to P. xylostella.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The paper shows rpx1-1 increases DMNT, reduces larval preference, increases mortality, lowers pupation, and RPX1 thus negatively affects DMNT-mediated defense.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point is supported in Results: rpx1-1 resistance phenotypes (Fig. 1), complementation restores susceptibility and low DMNT (Figs. 1 and 4), PEN1/DMNT increase (Figs. 3-5), protein degradation after infestation/wounding (Fig. 8), and higher seed yield under infestation without evident penalty (section 3.10).

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods were directly used for RPX1: T-DNA mutant, complementation, qRT-PCR, GC-MS, bioassays, and immunoblotting.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Section 3.1 states RPX1 encodes a novel cap-binding protein (nCBP); Discussion states it belongs to the eIF4E family.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Section 3.9 states RPX1 was expressed in different tissues (Supporting Information Figure S9a).

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Section 3.9 reports RPX1 expression in different tissues and no significant transcript response to 6-24 h larval infestation.

Applied_Promoters.reason: RPX1pro was used for complementation and RPX1-GFP; 35Spro was used for RPX1-FLAG overexpression.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: RPX1 was examined under P. xylostella infestation and mechanical wounding.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper shows a genetic/regulatory relationship between RPX1 and PEN1 expression, but does not demonstrate protein-protein interaction, nor call PEN1 an interacting protein.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Section 3.10 reports no obvious growth penalty in seed yield without infestation and much higher seed production of rpx1-1 under infestation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Results show DMNT can move from roots to aerial parts and is detected in leaves; PEN1 is mainly root expressed.

Omics_Data.reason: RNA-seq was performed and GEO accession GSE161940 is given in Data Availability.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Abstract, section 3.10, and Discussion explicitly propose RPX1 as useful for molecular breeding/genome editing to improve pest resistance without typical trade-offs.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Section 3.9 shows RPX1 protein but not transcript responds rapidly to infestation/wounding; Discussion says the exact mechanism of PEN1 regulation is unclear and may involve mRNA stability/translation.

corrections.reason: The paper shows a genetic/regulatory relationship between RPX1 and PEN1 expression, but does not demonstrate protein-protein interaction, nor call PEN1 an interacting protein.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Section 3.5 explicitly identifies PEN1 as At4g15340.

Applied_Species.reason: PEN1 transgenic analyses were performed in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: 35Spro:PEN1 was transformed into Col-0 and comparisons are in Col-0 background.

Source_Species.reason: PEN1 coding sequence was PCR-amplified from Col-0 cDNA.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The source species is Arabidopsis thaliana.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Constructs used Col-0 cDNA as source.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: RNA-seq/KEGG place PEN1 in sesquiterpenoid and triterpenoid biosynthesis, and the text states it leads to DMNT synthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper states PEN1 converts 2,3-oxidosqualene to arabidiol leading to DMNT via CYP705A1, but does not explicitly name this branch 'arabidiol-derived DMNT branch'.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Section 3.5 says PEN1 encodes an oxidosqualene cyclase; the abstract names it PENTACYCLIC TRITERPENE SYNTHASE 1.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided anywhere in the paper.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Section 3.5 states PEN1 converts 2,3-oxidosqualene to arabidiol.

Primary_Product.reason: The text explicitly states the product is arabidiol.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This description matches the statement that PEN1 converts 2,3-oxidosqualene to arabidiol and leads to bioactive DMNT via CYP705A1.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Because PEN1 forms arabidiol upstream of DMNT, calling it an upstream precursor-forming step is directly inferable from the paper.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The paper supports that PEN1 forms a precursor leading to DMNT rather than DMNT directly.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Sections 3.5-3.7 directly connect PEN1 manipulation with DMNT levels.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: DMNT is explicitly described as a homoterpene; this classification is consistent with the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: DMNT treatment and PEN1 overexpression/knockdown results support pest resistance via PM/midgut damage and larval death/repellence.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Figures 5 and 6 show higher PEN1 expression increases DMNT and resistance, while PEN1 knockdown in rpx1-1 suppresses both.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four summary points are supported by Results sections 3.6-3.7 and supporting figures.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These are exactly the methods used to test PEN1 in the Results.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Abstract calls PEN1 a key DMNT biosynthetic enzyme, and section 3.6 functionally supports a key role.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Section 3.5 explicitly identifies PEN1 as an oxidosqualene cyclase.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Section 3.8 and Supporting Figure S8a state PEN1 is mainly expressed in roots and very low in leaves; section 3.6 shows induction by infestation.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The paper states root-biased expression with very low leaf expression, and infestation induces PEN1 with higher expression in rpx1-1 after 24 h.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: P. xylostella infestation was used and shown to induce PEN1 expression.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper does not demonstrate protein-protein interactions for PEN1 with RPX1 or CYP705A1; CYP705A1 is cited as downstream in the pathway, not tested as an interactor.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Section 3.10 reports higher seed yield in 35Spro:PEN1 under infested and uninfested conditions; no obvious growth penalty is reported.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Results show DMNT is detected in aerial parts/leaves, while PEN1 expression is mainly in roots.

Omics_Data.reason: PEN1 emerged from RNA-seq and the GEO accession GSE161940 is provided.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Abstract, Results 3.10, and Discussion support PEN1 overexpression as useful for improving pest resistance without typical trade-offs.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly cites CYP705A1 as downstream for DMNT formation from arabidiol, and CYP705A1 was not experimentally tested in this study.

corrections.reason: No EC number is provided anywhere in the paper.
The paper does not demonstrate protein-protein interactions for PEN1 with RPX1 or CYP705A1; CYP705A1 is cited as downstream in the pathway, not tested as an interactor.
